narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have the door close like this feels deeply unfair, especially with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.2 and that full-time commitment you're ready to make. Our prediction model looked at your full picture and estimated a 60% chance of not passing the bar exam, which is why we had to make this tough call on your application. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, placing you at the very bottom of your class academically during that critical later stage, signaling to the model a steeper uphill battle ahead. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 18.0, which is on the lower side and pulled strongly toward this riskier outlook. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 added to that concern, showing early grades that, while not the worst, didn't build the momentum needed for bar success. Even with your middle-family income and Asian background—factors that can sometimes offer support—they didn't outweigh these academic signals in the model's view. Your gender as female and full-time status nudged things further toward caution, as patterns from past students with similar combos haven't always led to passing the bar. It's like the model saw your story as one where the grades dipped too low at key points, painting a picture of potential struggle despite your determination. This isn't about your worth or potential—it's a data-driven snapshot to protect you from years of stress without the payoff. Many students in your shoes have turned things around by targeting those weak spots, like boosting test prep or seeking grade recovery strategies before reapplying. You've got the drive; now it's about reshaping that story for next time. Hang in there—we're rooting for your comeback.
